nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on vertical rock faces and beneath overhangs of Red Sea coral reefs at depths of roughly 1–60 m, taking refuge in small holes and crevices when alarmed.

diet.notes: Generally considered an unfussy micropredator. Has been widely reported to readily accept most prepared aquarium foods, including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty seafood, and quality carnivore-formulated marine pellets or flakes. Has been documented preying on bristleworms and small flatworms in the display, which is often regarded as a useful side effect, but the same predatory behavior can extend to very small ornamental shrimp such as sexy shrimp. Generally advised to feed one or two small varied meals per day.

avoidWith: similarly shaped or coloured small fish

summary: A vivid purple Red Sea dottyback that is generally considered one of the most peaceful members of its family. Hardy, readily captive-bred, and beginner-friendly, but still semi-aggressive toward similar-shaped tankmates.
careNotes:
The Orchid Dottyback (Pseudochromis fridmani) is endemic to the Red Sea and is one of the longest-established captive-bred marine ornamentals — virtually all specimens in the trade today come from aquaculture facilities (ORA, Sea & Reef, Biota and others). The species is generally considered hardy, beginner-accessible, and one of the more peaceful dottybacks: BiomesInABox describes it as 'fairly peaceful by dottyback standards' and LiveAquaria categorises it as semi-aggressive, noting that it 'may occasionally chase smaller fish but will defend its hiding places'.

It is generally regarded as fully reef-safe with corals and most sessile invertebrates, though it has been reliably reported to prey on very small ornamental shrimp such as sexy shrimp; larger cleaner / fire shrimp typically coexist safely. The species is also widely documented as a useful natural predator of bristleworms and small flatworms.

Conspecific aggression is generally severe, as with most dottybacks — keep one per tank, and do not combine with other Pseudochromis species. An exception is that two very small individuals introduced simultaneously into a larger tank with abundant rockwork have been reported to pair-bond and form a breeding male/female pair, though success is not guaranteed.

A tight-fitting lid is generally advised — multiple care sources note the species is prone to jumping when startled. Decor should provide plenty of live rock with caves, crevices, and overhanging structures to mimic its preferred vertical-rockface habitat in the Red Sea.

Cross-source spread: max size — LiveAquaria reports 3 in (~7.6 cm), FishBase 6.3 cm TL; converged on 6–8 cm. Temperature — retail/hobby sources state 72–78°F (~22–26°C); FishBase wild range is 23.7–29.3°C with a mean of 26.7°C; averaged to 23–27°C. Salinity 1.020–1.025 SG and dKH 8–12 are universal across hobby sources. Minimum tank — LiveAquaria and BiomesInABox both state 30 US gal (~114 L); biased to 100 L per the tank-size rule, reflecting that this is one of the smaller and more peaceful dottybacks. Lifespan is not stated by the primary sources; 5–7 years is a conservative interpolation from general Pseudochromis longevity.

Named after David Fridman, the Israeli reef biologist who founded Coral World in Eilat. IUCN status: Least Concern (2019).
breedingNotes: Generally regarded as one of the easier marine fish to breed in captivity and one of the first species to be commercially aquacultured for the ornamental trade. Has been reliably bred by ORA, Sea & Reef Aquaculture, Biota and others. Adults are benthic cave-spawners: the male prepares a hiding site, courts a receptive female who deposits an egg ball inside the cave, and the male typically guards and tends the clutch until hatching. Home-aquarium breeding has been achieved by introducing two small individuals simultaneously into a large tank with abundant rockwork so they can pair-bond before maturing; conspecifics cannot otherwise be safely combined. Raising the planktonic larvae is considered moderately challenging and generally requires rotifers followed by enriched Artemia.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for confirmed scientific name (Pseudochromis fridmani), care level (Easy/beginner), temperament (Semi-Aggressive), reef-safe (Yes), diet (carnivore — varied carnivore-formulated foods, bristleworm/mantis-shrimp predator), max size (3 in / ~7.6 cm), minimum tank (30 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), family Pseudochromidae, Red Sea origin, captive-bred availability, and the husbandry note that multiple specimens (if attempted) must be added simultaneously. Reached via the /collections/dottybacks index.

Source for the explicit characterisation of this species as 'fairly peaceful by dottyback standards', for the jumping/escape risk that justifies escapeRisk: high and the tight-fitting-lid recommendation, and for the pair-bonding behaviour when two small individuals are introduced together into a large tank.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawning species; the male prepares a hiding site, courts a receptive female who deposits an egg ball inside, and the male typically guards the clutch. One of the most commercially aquacultured marine ornamentals (ORA, Sea & Reef, Biota). Home-aquarium breeding is feasible by introducing two small individuals simultaneously into a large, rockwork-rich tank so they can pair-bond before maturing — conspecifics otherwise cannot be safely combined. Larvae are planktonic and require rotifers followed by enriched Artemia; rearing is moderately challenging but well documented.
